result:
I keep a list of things that have no name.

Not “nameless” in the romantic, unsayable-beauty sense—more like the moment when your palm is hovering over the stove coil and you haven’t decided whether you’ll touch it. That pause: not hesitation, not curiosity, something else. I write it down as “pre-burn audition.” The list lives in the back of a 2009 planner I stole from an office job. Every January I date the first page “Year 0001” and then never add another calendarial mark again; the rest is only the list. Item #4: the sound of the upstairs neighbor’s vacuum at 2:13 a.m. when you realize it’s actually a massage gun, or maybe grief. Item #112: the exact color of the sky the day you understood your parents would die in the same order they were born. Item #337: the smell of rain on hot asphalt translated into the language your great-grandmother stopped speaking after the boat docked. The planner’s fake leather is flaking like old propaganda posters; the flakes stick to my fingers so that I carry the colors of an obsolete future on my skin for hours.

Sometimes I read the list aloud to the dark. The words feel heavier without witnesses, like smuggled ore. I imagine each entry as a bead on a necklace strung across the throat of something huge and asleep. If I finish reciting before the sun edges the curtain, the year will reset and nobody I love will die. I have failed every time so far, but failure has its own incense; I keep breathing it, a devotee of almost.

Yesterday I tried to add #561: the expression on the barista’s face when I said “surprise me” and she realized she was too tired to be surprised. But the pen died mid-sentence. Ink hemorrhaged into the margin, a Rorschach of thwarted confession. I stared at it until the blob looked like a map of every city I’ve left before the roads could memorize me. Then I tore the page out, folded it into a paper crane, and set it on the windowsill. Overnight the rain softened it into a pulp dove. This morning I found it gone—only a damp outline remapping the wood. I choose to believe it flew, though the neighborhood crows insist on breakfast evidence.

In the absence of new entries I start interviewing strangers for replacements. I carry a cardboard sign that reads “Tell me the thing you can’t tell anyone else, I’ll give it a name, no charge.” Most keep walking, earbuds armor-plated with Spotify’s curated courage. A woman with grocery bags stopped, though. Her eyes were the color of airport carpets—designed to hide every stain. She said: “When my daughter laughs in her sleep I feel accused of happiness.” I wrote that down as #561a, provisional. She wanted to know the name I’d give it. I offered: “lucid joy indictment.” She shook her head, said naming was violence, took her mangoes and left. I watched the bags swing like heavy chandeliers in the mansion of her disappearing.

At dusk I walk to the abandoned drive-in where the screen collapsed into itself mid-reel decades ago. The frame still stands, a dinosaur ribcage against the purple. I project the list onto it with a flashlight whose lens is cracked; the words fracture, reassemble into constellations no mythology has plagiarized yet. Item #88: the weight of unread books increasing gravity beside your bed. Item #201: the precise second a hug begins its withdrawal. When the beam wavers, the entire sky seems to pixelate, as though reality buffers. I stand in the tall grass applauding the lag, encore, encore.

One night a boy appeared on the hood of a corroded Buick, hoodie drawn closed like a curtain before the play is ready. He asked if I was the census taker for missing moments. I told him I was more like their foster parent until they grew up and moved out of language. He said he had one that fit: “the echo of my mother’s heartbeat on the ultrasound recording, which I stole from her nightstand and lost before I was born.” I wrote it as #600, a round number, auspicious. He wanted to listen to it. I held the planner up to his ear; he closed his eyes so long I thought maybe he’d traveled inside. When he opened them he looked younger, like someone had pressed rewind on his cells. He said thanks, jumped down, walked into the corn that isn’t corn anymore—just biofuel stubble. I never saw him leave, only heard the stalks gossiping about the weather inside a person.

The list is now 612 entries. I carry it like a horizontal hourglass: when it reaches 1,000 the world will tip over and start counting backward. People will un-say their last goodbyes, cancers will revise their eviction notices, every war will refund its dead like faulty appliances. I suspect this is a private superstition, but superstitions are just unlicensed prayers, and prayers are unpaid poems, and poems—according to the government—are optional, so here we are, optional together.

Last week the planner tried to add itself to the list. While I slept it inscribed #613 in my own handwriting: “the moment the list realizes it is heavier than the life it catalogs.” I woke to find the coil bleeding metal threads, pages bulging like overfed lungs. Panic tasted of cheap office coffee. I tore out the offending entry, ate it page and all. The paper dissolved on my tongue into a sermon of pulp. For twenty-four hours I spoke only in soft, unfinished clauses. Strangers patted my shoulder, nodded as if I were delivering weather forecasts for a country they’d fled.

Now I stand at the stove again, palm aloft. The coil glows its tangerine sermon. I could record the sensation—#614: the temperature at which language decides skin is the more honest tongue—but the planner is almost full, and the year is ending, and the necklace of unnamed things tightens by one bead each midnight. I hover, I hover, I—

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
